coredata.dc:title: Enhanced hydrogen production by robust covalent biohybrid based on cell membrane specific click chemistry

coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Tian-Yu
Zhao, Yi-Cheng
Sha, Chong
Nawab, Said
Liu, Jun-Ying
Yong, Yang-Chun
coredata.dc:description: The light-driven semiconductor-bacteria hybrid holds great potential for synthesis of diverse solar chemicals and fuels. However, the efficiency of electron transfer between biotic-abiotic interface often suffers from the insufficient robustness of biohybrid, which significantly imped its performance and applications. Here, a highly robust biohybrid was established by specifically and covalently grafting carbon quantum dots (CDs) onto bacterial cell membrane via the copper-catalyzed azide-Alkyne click (CuAAC) reaction. The formation of covalent bonds dramatically enhanced the robustness of hybrid and further shorten the distance of biotic-abiotic interface, endowing long-term stability under different conditions. Consequently, the cell membrane-specific covalent-biohybrid exhibited a 7.3-fold higher hydrogen production due to enhanced system stability, higher load of CDs on the cell surface and direct electron transfer efficiency. This work demonstrated a new and promising approach to improve the robustness and performance of photocatalytic semiconductor-bacteria hybrid system, which would further diversify the toolbox for solar chemicals/fuels production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Interfacial electron transfer
Photocatalytic biohybrid
Hydrogen production
Click chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots
Preparing the alkynyl groups modified carbon quantum dots
N3-Labeling Shewanella oneidensis by GalNAz and identification
Calculation of the amount of carbon quantum dots bound to Shewanella oneidensis
Construction of covalent biohybrid with cell membrane specific click chemistry
Improved robustness of biohybrid by cell membrane covalent binding
Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production by covalent biohybrid
Enhanced direct electron transfer in biohybrid by covalent binding

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Construction of covalent-biohybrid system with cell membrane specific click chemistry. (a) Flowchart of covalent click biohybrid system construction; (b) TEM image of carbon quantum dots (CDs); (c) Particle size distribution map of carbon quantum dots (CDs); (d) FTIR spectra of carbon quantum dots modified with carboxyl groups (CDs-COOH) and carbon quantum dots modified with alkynyl groups (CDs-Alkyne); (e) TEM of pure Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 (SO); (f) TEM of the click biohybrid system; (g) FTIR spectra of two biohybrid systems.

The loading of carbon quantum dots on the Shewanella oneidensis. (a) The amount of carbon quantum dots (CDs) binding to cells after mixing and the “click” reaction; (b) The highest hydrogen production for different starting concentrations of carbon quantum dots (CDs) by the covalent click system.

System stability of biohybrid by cell membrane covalent binding. (a) The fluorescence intensity of two biohybrid systems within 6 days; (b) The fluorescence intensity of two biohybrid systems under different pH conditions.

Electrochemical characterization. (a) I-t curves of turnover currents of simple mixing, click, and carbon quantum dots (CDs) electrodes under light; (b) CV curve of Shewanella oneidensis, simple mixing, and click system, scanning rate of 10 mV s−1.

Continuous H2 production of Shewanella oneidensis, carbon quantum dots modified with carboxyl groups (CDs-COOH), carbon quantum dots modified with alkynyl groups (CDs-Alkyne), Shewanella oneidensis and carbon quantum dots modified with carboxyl groups (SO + CDs-COOH mixing), Shewanella oneidensis and carbon quantum dots modified with alkynyl groups (SO + CDs-Alkyne mixing), and click system.

Electron transfer mechanism. (a) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of bare carbon quantum dots (CDs), mixing, click and Shewanella oneidensis (SO) (λex = 337 nm). (b) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of carbon quantum dots (CDs), mixing, and click, fitting with biexponential decay functions (solid lines). (c) Effects of addition of riboflavin on the hydrogen production by simple mixing and covalent click system at 20 h. P values were determined by ordinary one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s multiple comparisons test (n = 3 biologically independent samples). ***, P < 0.01. ns (not significant), P > 0.05. (d) Effects of different inhibitors on the hydrogen production at 20 h. (e) Hydrogen production by c-cytochrome knockout bacteria (ΔMtrC, ΔMtrA, ΔMtrC/ΔOmcA) at 20 h as well as wild type Shewanella oneidensis (SO) after “click” reaction. (f) Hydrogen production by hydrogenase knockout strain (ΔHydA, ΔHyaB, ΔHydA/ΔHyaB) as well as the wild type Shewanella oneidensis (SO) after “click” reaction. (g) Electron transport mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen production by two types of hybrid systems, covalent click and simple mixing biohybrid systems.

head.ce:title: Enhanced hydrogen production by robust covalent biohybrid based on cell membrane specific click chemistry

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The light-driven semiconductor-bacteria hybrid holds great potential for synthesis of diverse solar chemicals and fuels. However, the efficiency of electron transfer between biotic-abiotic interface often suffers from the insufficient robustness of biohybrid, which significantly imped its performance and applications. Here, a highly robust biohybrid was established by specifically and covalently grafting carbon quantum dots (CDs) onto bacterial cell membrane via the copper-catalyzed azide-Alkyne click (CuAAC) reaction. The formation of covalent bonds dramatically enhanced the robustness of hybrid and further shorten the distance of biotic-abiotic interface, endowing long-term stability under different conditions. Consequently, the cell membrane-specific covalent-biohybrid exhibited a 7.3-fold higher hydrogen production due to enhanced system stability, higher load of CDs on the cell surface and direct electron transfer efficiency. This work demonstrated a new and promising approach to improve the robustness and performance of photocatalytic semiconductor-bacteria hybrid system, which would further diversify the toolbox for solar chemicals/fuels production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen has attracted more and more attention because of its advantages of high efficiency, clean and renewable, which is expected to account for 90 % of global fuel by 2080 (da Silva Veras et al., 2017). Photocatalytic biohybrid system, as an emerging technical platforms, integrates the superior light harvesting properties of semiconductors with the biosynthetic capabilities of biological cells or enzymes, thereby establishing a novel and efficient platform for chemical synthesis (Bachar et al., 2024; Bachar et al., 2022; Guo et al., 2018; Pi et al., 2023), carbon dioxide reduction (Badiani et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2022), and nitrogen fixation (Brown et al., 2016; Meng et al., 2021). The energy flow of photosynthetic biohybrid system mainly includes three key steps: solar energy capture, cross-membrane electron transport and energy conversion into different chemicals (Liang et al., 2024). While the efficiency of cross-membrane electron transfer primarily relies on the electron transfer speed and even the proximity between biotic and abiotic surfaces (Bian et al., 2024). Therefore, enhancing electron transfer speed from the nanomaterials to cells is crucial for developing high-performance photocatalytic biohybrid platforms.

The binding modes or the interaction of typical hybrids for cells and nanomaterials primarily encompass random collision as well as cell surface localization (Liang et al., 2024). Under the simple mixing system, some electron shuttles could transport electrons from nanomaterials to cell membrane. However, under these conditions, the photoelectrons must be promptly transported to electron carriers; otherwise, they will recombine with the oxidative holes (h+) of the semiconductor. For the cell surface localization, nanomaterials could be fixed to the microbial surface mainly through non-covalent bonds, such as electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, and hydrogen bond. Gai et al. constructed a p-n heterojunction layer as the photosensitizers through electrostatic interactions and hydrophobic interactions, which were coated on the bacteria surface to produce acetic acid from CO2 under light (Gai et al., 2020). However, the performance of these biohybrids depending on the non-covalent bonds could heavily be influenced by various factors including pH level, ion strength, and others, which exhibit lower biohybrid stability. Additionally, semi-conductive CdS/CdSe nanoparticles, as the typical photosensitive nanomaterials, could be coated on the surface of bacteria by self-assembly method to form the bacteria@CdS/CdSe biohybrid, which proved to be an efficient catalyst to produce H2 (Edwards et al., 2023), CH4 (Wang et al., 2024), and other chemicals (He et al., 2022; Sakimoto et al., 2016). Until now, there are a few of species capable of achieving self-precipitated nanoparticles on their cell surfaces, such as Methanosarcina barkeri (Wang et al., 2024), Moorella thermoacetica (Sakimoto et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2018), Sporomusa ovata (He et al., 2022), Shewanella oneidensis (Martins et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2023), Citrobacter freundii (Liu et al., 2023), Rhodobacter capsulatus (Li et al., 2024b), Escherichia coli (Xiao et al., 2021), and even yeast (Li et al., 2018).

In current biohybrid systems, the predominant interaction mode between nanomaterials and cells relies on non-covalent bond. However, covalent bond-click chemistry, including azide-alkyne cycloaddition catalyzed with or without the copper, has emerged as a cornerstone strategy in synthetic biotechnology, nanotechnology, and materials science due to its high efficiency in modular synthesis and bioconjugation (Hong et al., 2009). Bacteria containing promiscuous metabolic pathways can uptake unnatural monosaccharides and incorporate them into the glycans of cell membrane by metabolic oligosaccharide engineering (MOE), such as GlcNAz, GalNAz, KdoAz and so on (Luong & Dube, 2021). Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 (SO), a Gram-negative bacterium, is able to use its own metabolic pathway to incorporate the azide-modified sugars (GalNAz) into capsular polysaccharide of the cell membrane surface (Zhao et al., 2024). Recent advancements have further developed living cell click chemistry, successfully enhancing direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) efficiency (Zhao et al., 2024). Nevertheless, the performance of photocatalytic biohybrid systems constructed via this cutting-edge approach remains unexplored, representing a critical knowledge gap in current research.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as “green” organic semiconductors, exhibit excellent biocompatibility in biological systems (Lv et al., 2024). Due to the tunable surface chemistry of CDs (Aggarwal et al., 2024), alkynyl groups were easily introduced onto the surface of CDs (Fig. 1 a). Meanwhile, the azide-modified sugars were successfully integrated onto the cell membrane surface via the metabolic system of the cells (Zhao et al., 2024). Subsequently, a robust and efficient biohybrid system was achieved by covalently binding CDs to the cell membrane surface through the CuCCA reaction. The efficient production of solar H2 has thus been achieved without the need for an organic carbon source. The electron transfer mechanism of the membrane-specific click biohybrid system will also be systematically investigated in this manuscript.

AZDye 488 DBCO was procured from Xi’an Qiyue Biology CO. LTD (China). BTTAA was obtained from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical CO. LTD (China). GalNAz (Tetraacylated N-acetylglucosamine) was acquired from Xi'an Kaixin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. LTD (China). LIVE/DEAD® BacLight™ Bacterial Viability Kit was supplied by Thermo Fisher Scientific (US). All chemicals were sourced from commercial suppliers and utilized without further purification unless otherwise stated. Additional organic and inorganic chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Group Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). S. oneidensis MR-1 (ATCC700550) was obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and conserved in lab.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carboxylic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature, carboxylic acid-functionalized CDs were synthesized by citric acid thermal decomposition method (Hutton et al., 2016). In brief, citric acid (100 g) was pyrolysed (muffle furnace) in air at 180 °C for 40 h to produce carboxylic acid capped CDs in the form of an orange-brown highly viscous liquid. Initially the citric acid melted to form a colourless liquid, which then gradually changed colour from yellow/orange to brown. The orange-brown high viscosity liquid was dissolved with deionised water (100 mL) and aqueous NaOH (5 M, 50 mL) with stirring. More aqueous NaOH (5 M, ∼25 mL) was subsequently added to neutralise the acidic CDs to pH 7.0, resulting in an orange-brown solution of sodium carboxylate capped CDs (CDs-COOH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparing the alkynyl groups modified carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-Alkyne nanocomposites were prepared depending on the amidation (Hutton et al., 2016). In brief, to prepare CDs-Alkyne nanocomposites, 1.0 mg of CDs-COOH was dispersed in 1 mL of 10 mM MES buffer (pH 5.5) and activated with 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC, 40 mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 10 mM) for 4 h at room temperature. The product was washed with water and dispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4). 100 μL activated CDs-COOH was added to 1.9 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 2.0 mg of NH2-PEG-Alkyne and shaken at room temperature for 2 h. The final product was stored for further use at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N3-Labeling Shewanella oneidensis by GalNAz and identification
ce:section.ce:para: SO were incubated at 30 °C for 12 h in LB medium including 2 mM GalNAz and washed three times with PBS buffer (0.05 M, pH 7.4) with 1 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) at 25 °C and centrifugated at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The strain was further resuspended in PBS buffers with 1 % BSA and added 2 μl of 2.5 mM AZDye 488 DBCO. The tubes were incubated (30 °C, 200 rpm shaking and protected from light) for 1 h, and then cells were washed 3 times with 3 % BSA in PBS buffers at 5000 rpm for 5 min at 25 °C. After a final wash, cells were fixed and mounted on a microscope slide for viewing.

The experimental procedures were conducted under distinct conditions. For the covalent click system, SO-N3 (OD600 = 1) were resuspended in 500 μL reaction mixture containing 2 mg/mL CDs-Alkyne, 1 μL of 50 mM CuSO4, 2.5 μL of 100 mM BTTAA, 100 μL of 25 mM Vc and 400 μL of sterile ddH2O. In parallel, for the simple mixing system, unlabeled SO cells (OD600 = 1) were treated with 2 mg/mL CDs-COOH in PBS buffer (pH 7.4). Both reaction systems were protected from light and incubated at room temperature for 1 h. Post-incubation, cells underwent three successive PBS washing cycles followed by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 5 min, 25 °C) to remove unbound nanoparticles. The cell precipitation would further be resuspended in tris buffer (pH 7.0) for load calculation and hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the amount of carbon quantum dots bound to Shewanella oneidensis
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of covalent click or simple mixing biohybrid was used to indicate the amount of CDs on SO. SO-N3 or SO (OD600 = 1) was dissolved in different concentration of CDs-Alkyne or CDs-COOH (0. 025 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, 0.25 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 2.5 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL), respectively. The fluorescence intensity released from samples of different concentrations of CDs mixed with SO was used as the standard curve. The hybrid was washed three times with 3 % BSA in PBS buffer to remove CDs which were not attached to SO. The amount of validly attached CDs on SO was based on the fluorescence intensity. The hybrid was excited at 337 nm and the emission wavelength was 460 nm.

Photocatalytic hydrogen production was followed as before, which were performed by light irradiation in a homemade photocatalytic device where 5 mL scintillation vials were placed (Luo et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2020). For the hydrogenase expression in SO or SO-N3, until the OD600 reached 0.6 ∼ 0.8, the bacterial suspension obtained was poured into a 500 mL anaerobic serum bottle with LB and 3-Morpholinopropanesulfoinc Acid (MOPs) medium (5 g/L yeast extract, 10 g/L tryptone, 10 g/L NaCl, 100 mM MOPs, pH 7. 2). Then 20 mM sodium lactate, 10 mM cysteine, 25 mM sodium sulphate, and 2 mM GalNAz (for the click system) were added, and the entire bacterial solution was deoxidized for 30 min by N2. Finally, the bottles are strictly sealed, and hydrogenase expression is incubated at a constant incubation (30 °C, 200 rpm) for 20 h.

The SO cells hydrogenase activated (with or without GalNAz labeling) were washed three times with PBS buffer. SO-N3 were subjected to the click reaction according to the system construction method described above (method 2.5. Construction of two biohybrid systems), whereas the control group of simple mixing was made by mixing the CDs directly with the cells. Hydrogen production contains 10 mM tris buffer (pH 7.0), 150 mM NaCl, and 10 mM sodium sulfite. 10 mM sodium sulfite was used as a sacrificial electron donor to capture photogenerated holes. The power of each LED was set to 45 W ± 5 W, as measured with a digital lux meter (TES 1332A, Tes Electrical Electronic Corp, China) placed over the photocatalytic device. Photocatalytic hydrogen production was detected by sampling the headspace via gas chromatography (GC 7900Ⅱ, Shanghai Techomp Scientific Instrument Co., Shanghai, China) using high-purity N2 as the carrier gas (three independent replications were performed for each set of data).

The LIVE/DEAD BacLight Bacterial Viability Kit was used to detect potential toxic effects of copper-catalyzed click chemistry, different concentration of CDs, and the stability of two biohybrids (Zhao et al., 2024). After click chemistry, the cells were collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min, washed with 0.9 % NaCl for three times and resuspended in 0.9 % NaCl solution. Mixed staining solution with an equal volume of the cell suspension and then incubated in the dark for 10 min. The stained cells were observed by fluorescence microscopy (MF-31, Guangzhou Mshot Photoelectric Technology CO., LTD., China). Green fluorescence indicated live cell, while red fluorescence indicated dead cell.

CDs were resuspended in an appropriate amount of ethanol, sonicated for 20 min and then dripped onto a copper grid for observation using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100 PLUS, Jeol Ltd., Japan) at 200 kV. Pure SO and click samples were collected by centrifugation, washing, and resuspension in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde overnight. The samples were then washed three times with ddH2O and freeze-dried (BERR 1–8 LD, Beijing Bolixing Instrument Company, Beijing, China). The dried sample powder was resuspended with appropriate amount of ethanol and dropped on a copper grid, and the TEM images were taken using a TEM (HT7800, Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Ltd., Japan) at 120 kV. Cell imaging was performed with a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP5, Leica, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) analyses were obtained by a Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer. The XRD measurement was conducted by X-ray powder diffusion tester D/MAX-2500. The UV–vis diffusion reflectivity spectroscopy analysis (DRS) was recorded by the UV-2450 spectrometer (Shimada, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained with the Thermo ESCALAB 250X (American) electron spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed on Perkin-Elmer LS 55.

For the stability and pH tolerance of two biohybrid systems, the CDs load is used to characterize the stability of the systems. After the mixing and click reaction, the samples were subjected to centrifugation and followed by resuspension. Subsequently, their fluorescence intensities were quantified as the initial fluorescence intensity values. The aforementioned procedure was conducted on a daily basis, wherein the samples were consistently enveloped in tinfoil throughout the entirety of the process. For the pH tolerance, the system's stability was assessed under varying pH environments. One day later, the percentage of residual fluorescence intensity was utilized to quantify the load of CDs on the cell surface.

Anaerobic electrochemistry was carried out in a quartz chamber equipped with a CHI 660E electrochemical station and a three-electrode device (Liu et al., 2020). For the photocurrent transient responses of different biohybrid system, the working electrode used in the experiments was a glass plate coated with indium tin oxide (ITO), located at the bottom of the chamber where the self-precipitated samples were located. A platinum sheet (1x1 cm) was used as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl (KCl saturated) was used as the reference electrode. Mott–Schottky measurements were operated in a standard three-electrode system with Ag/AgCl (KCl saturated) electrode as the reference electrode and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The as-prepared products were used as the working electrodes. 12 mL M9 buffer (pH 7.0, Na2HPO4·12H2O, 42 mM KH2PO4, 22 mM MgSO4, 1 mM CaCl2, 0.1 mM and NaCl, 85.5 mM) containing sodium lactate (18 mM) was injected into the reactor as electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of covalent biohybrid with cell membrane specific click chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the robustness and tightness for biotic/abiotic interfaces of biohybrid, “click chemistry” between azides and alkynes was designed and employed to form covalent biohybrid system. The synthesis and characterization of alkyne-functionalized CDs were depicted in Fig. 1a. In brief, the carboxyl groups of the CDs were activated using NHS/EDC, and the introduction of the alkyne group was achieved through an amidation reaction. The azide-modified SO was first stained with AZDye 488 DBCO, and the results showed that the SO surface was successfully modified with azide groups (see Supplementary Material, Fig. S1).

CDs were selected as the photosensitive semiconductor for the biohybrid system due to the abundance of functional groups on the surfaces, which rendered it exceedingly amenable to modifications (Aggarwal et al., 2024; Cailotto et al., 2018). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed a diameter distribution of the resulting CDs ranging from 1-8 nm, with an average size of approximately 4 nm, indicating excellent size uniformity (Fig. 1b and 1c). CDs exhibits excellent solubility, emitting a bright blue luster under UV light irradiation, and the fluorescence intensifies with increasing concentration (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S2a and S2b). In addition, UV visible spectroscopy and fluorescence excitation emission spectroscopy confirmed the successful synthesis of quantum dots and showed a slight red shift phenomenon due to the large number of –COOH on the surface of CDs, when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S2c and Fig. S2d) (Chen et al., 2024; Hutton et al., 2016; Park et al., 2016). CDs exhibited a strong PL emission peak centered at 460 nm, with mirror symmetry between the maximum excitation band and the maximum emission band. However, this emission depends on the excitation wavelength, and as the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak shifts towards a longer wavelength as the intensity decreases (Wu et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2023). XRD spectra displayed similar crystalline structure of CDs with or without alkyne group (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S2e). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) revealed the presence of characteristic carbon triple bond in the CDs after modification of alkynylation (Fig. 1d). The CDs-Alkyne exhibit a stretching peak at 2100 cm−1 corresponding to CC, along with stretching vibrational regions at 3440 cm−1, 2917 cm−1, 1625 cm−1, and 1093 cm−1 for OH, CH, CO, and CO, respectively. Thus, the CDs containing the alkyne group (CDs-Alkyne) have been successfully synthesized. To further investigate the surface chemical structure and composition of the CDs-Alkyne, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted. The XPS results (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S3) confirmed the presence of C, N and O in the CDs-Alkyne, but only C and O in the CDs.

Then the construction of a membrane-specific hybrid system (Click) was achieved by the combination of SO-N3 and CDs-Alkyne through click chemistry (Fig. 1a). To further investigate the microstructure of biohybrid Click, the TEM characterization was conducted (Fig. 1e and 1f). Compared to pure SO (Fig. 1e), the intact bacterial contour is depicted in Fig. 1f, indicating that CDs has minimal impact on the morphology of Shewanella. Furthermore, TEM images also demonstrated the successful construction of the click hybrid system, with CDs uniformly and densely decorated on the bacterial surface. After the click reaction, SO-N3 and CDs-Alkyne will be covalently linked through 1,2,3-triazoles, showed representative absorption bands near 1550, 1450, 1250, 1050, and 750 cm−1, while the mixing system did not show obvious representative absorption bands (Fig. 1g) (Hashidzume et al., 2013). The determination of bacterial viability in the hybrid system after the “click” reaction is also illustrated. The results showed that the click reaction had less effects on cell viability (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S4a). When the concentration of CDs was below 5 mg/ml, the cell viability exceeded 90 % (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S4b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improved robustness of biohybrid by cell membrane covalent binding
ce:section.ce:para: The correlation between the quantity of CDs added in the “click” system and its adsorption onto the cell surface in the “mixing” system is depicted in Fig. 2 a. The curve is obtained through nonlinear fitting using the Langmuir formula. The quantity of CDs-Alkyne binding to the cell surface in the click system is not expected to exhibit a significant increase beyond 1 mg/ml, as observed. Moreover, when 1 mg/mL of CDs were added, the load of CDs in the click system is about 3 times higher than that in the mixing system. The hydrogen production was also investigated under different starting CDs concentration in the click system. As shown in Fig. 2b, the hydrogen production reached its maximum when the reaction between SO-N3 and CDs-Alkyne was carried out at a concentration of 2 mg/mL CDs using the “click” reaction. The higher starting concentration of CDs would probably induce cellular mortality (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S4). While the lower starting concentration of CDs would decrease the load of CDs on the cell membrane (Fig. 2a) and hydrogen production (Fig. 2b). Therefore, an initial concentration of 2 mg/mL CDs-Alkyne would be employed for all the subsequent experiments in the click system.

The stability and pH tolerance were further employed to assess the performance of the two hybrid systems. Firstly, CDs and SO or CDs-Alkyne and SO-N3 were mixed at an initial concentration of 2 mg/ml for both kinds of CDs. The percentage of residual fluorescence intensity was utilized to quantify the load of CDs on the cell surface. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the click system remained at 93 % after a duration of six days. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the simple mixing reaction system decreased to 31 % (Fig. 3 a). In addition, the effects of retention days on the cell viability were also investigated. Only on the sixth day, the cell survival rate decreased to less than 80 % for both biohybrids in the PBS buffer under pH 7.0 (see Supplementary Materials, Fig. S5). All the results demonstrate the remarkable covalent structure stability of the hybrid system synthesized via click reaction. The system's stability was also assessed under varying pH environments. After reaction, the solution was subsequently adjusted to various pH values, followed by centrifugation and resuspension in order to quantify the fluorescence intensity of the samples. The fluorescence intensity of the simple mixing system is halved compared to that of the covalent click biohybrid system at pH 7, indicating an approximately 50 % reduction in the load of CDs compared to the click system (Fig. 3b). The results also revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the click hybrid system remained stable within the range of 70–95 % in different pH environment. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of the system formed by simple adsorption was merely 12 % at pH 12. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of pure CDs exhibited no significant variation (data not shown). These finding demonstrated the superior stability of the covalent click hybrid system in varying pH conditions.

Subsequently, the properties of two different hybrid systems were investigated by electrochemical characterization. Assessing the real-time response, the amperometric I-t curve in Fig. 4 a illustrates that irradiation significantly induced an enhancement in photocurrent at the click electrode. These findings indicated that the light could enhance the biocatalytic behavior of click biohybrid system, potentially due to the photosensitivity of the close interconnected CDs and SO (Liu et al., 2020). Finally, the CV curves show negligible changes in SO cells, and the covalent click system shows a much larger capacitive current, which could be attributed to the enhanced conductivity of click system (Fig. 4b) (Gai et al., 2020). All these results demonstrated that the covalent click biohybrid exhibited long-term stability and enhanced electrochemical activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production by covalent biohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: SO could express ecto [NiFe]-hydrogenase (HyaB) and ecto [FeFe]-hydrogenase (HydA) (Luo et al., 2021). To further evaluate the hydrogen production performance of different biohybrid systems, the hydrogen gas yield was continuously monitored. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the hydrogen gas production exhibited nearly linear growth for 10 h and plateaued after 36 h. Among them, the hydrogen production rate in the covalent click hybrid system, established through the click reaction between CDs-Alkyne and SO-N3, was approximately 7.3 times higher than that of the simple mixing system, while the load of CDs on the cell membrane in the click system was only 3.2 times higher than that of the simple mixing system. Lou et al. (Lou et al., 2024) reported that the covalent construction of laccase and metal–organic framework reduced the distance of the active site from 27.8 Å to 18.1 Å. This suggests that covalent bonding at the ultra-close contact interface between biological and non-biological components effectively enhanced the electron transfer efficiency, aligning with previous reports on successful inter-species electron transfer achievements (Zhao et al., 2024). In fact, the performance of our hybrid system falls behind that of other hybrid systems utilizing carbon source (glucose) fermentation (Li et al., 2024a; Wu et al., 2022). However, in biohybrid systems without carbon source fermentation, despite only organic carbon materials was used, the hydrogen production activity is much higher than that of metal materials. For example, the H2 production of biohybrid systems SO-CdS reached 14.4 µmol/gdcw/h, which was much lower than that of this covalent click biohybrid system (Martins et al., 2021). The photocatalytic H2 production of Cu2O/RGO/SO reached 80 µmol/gCu2O/h under visible-light irradiation, which was also lower than 180 µmol/gCDs/h of this click system (Shen et al., 2020). The research findings collectively demonstrate that the covalently bound hybrid system constructed in this research significantly enhanced the hydrogen production, surpassing even that of the metal-containing photocatalytic material@cell hybrid system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced direct electron transfer in biohybrid by covalent binding
ce:section.ce:para: The electron transfer mechanism in the three photocatalytic systems, namely pure SO system, simple mixing system and covalent click system, was first investigated by photoluminescence spectroscopy (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence intensity of pure SO cells is negligible. Compared to bare CDs, the PL intensity for the simple mixing system slightly decreased, while the PL intensity of the click system decreased significantly. This indicates that the hydrogenase in click system accepts more electrons, leading to enhanced inhibition of electron-hole recombination. Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy revealed that the fluorescence lifetime of click system was longer than that of pure CDs and mixing system, indicating efficient separation and transfer of photoelectrons into the cells (Fig. 6b) (Luo et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the electron transport mode of two hybrid systems were also analyzed (Fig. 6c). Upon the addition of riboflavin to the reaction system, a significant increase in hydrogen production (by ∼ 65 %) was observed in the simple mixing system, while no substantial change was observed in the click system (Fig. 6c). The dominance of indirect electron transfer has been confirmed as the primary mode of electron transfer between the biological/abiotic interface in the simple mixing system with the addition of riboflavin, while direct electron transfer was obviously enhanced in the covalent click biohybrid system. The phenomenon bears resemblance to the previously identified electron transfer occurring between different species (Zhao et al., 2024).

Furthermore, the electron transfer mechanism between CDs and SO cells was also studied by utilizing electron-transfer inhibitors of c-type cytochromes (Zang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). The hydrogen production in the click biohybrid system significantly decreased after the addition of inhibitors, rotenone and dicoumarol/capsaicin, suggesting that electrons are transferred into the cells through the membrane protein pathway, such as MtrCAB or MtrDEF (Fig. 6d). The main electron transport pathway of the click biohybrid system was further validated through the utilization of some c-cytochrome knockout bacteria (Fig. 6e). The results showed that the MtrC/OmcA double mutant extremely inhibited the H2 production (ΔMtrC inhibited ∼ 28 %, ΔMtrA inhibited ∼ 11 %, and ΔMtrC/OmcA inhibited ∼ 11 %), which was in good agreement with the previous reports that OmcA could partially compensate for the extracellular electronic transfer (EET) functions of MtrC. The compensation of MtrC by OmcA or other c-cytochromes were largely varied depends on the experimental conditions, such as the reduction of Cr (VI) (Belchik et al., 2011), the reduction of ferrihydrite (Han et al., 2018), the reduction of riboflavin (Coursolle et al., 2010) and so on. Therefore, the transmembrane electron transport pathway of the constructed covalent click biohybrid system primarily relies on the MtrCAB pathway.

To gain further insights into the role of hydrogenase in the click biohybrid system and elucidate the electron transfer mechanism, a series of genetically modified strains with hydrogenase knockout were engineered in SO cells, followed by an investigation into their impact on H2 production. As shown in Fig. 6f, the single-gene deletions of HydA or HyaB reduced the H2 production to 36 μmol/g/h and 24 μmol/g/h, respectively. The results indicated that [FeFe] hydrogenase and [NiFe] hydrogenase play a dominant role in hydrogen production. For the double deletion mutant of HydA and HyaB, the H2 production significantly decreased to 5.7 μmol/g/h, much lower than that of the single-gene deletion mutants of HydA or HyaB. The results of these experiments suggested that these hydrogenases could function as highly efficient active sites for hydrogen production in the covalent click biohybrid system.

Finally, a covalent biohybrid system via cell membrane specific click chemistry between CDs and membrane of SO was constructed (Fig. 6g). CDs functioned as semiconductor electron collectors, while the hydrogenase in SO served as a catalytic conversion unit in the periplasmic space. Compared to the simple mixing system, more CDs were covalently bound to the extracellular side of the cell membrane and the distance between the interface between CDs and SO will be further shorten in the click biohybrid system, resulting into high direct electron transfer efficiency. On the contrary, the electron transfer for the mixing system probably depended on the random collision between CDs and SO. Furthermore, owing to the substantial loading of CDs in the click biohybrid system, an increased number of electrons are transferred into the periplasmic space, consequently enhancing hydrogen production.

The present study successfully established a membrane-specific biohybrid system, through the covalent linkage of carbon quantum dots to Shewanella oneidensis utilizing the click reaction. The formation of covalent bond between cells and nanoparticles could increase the stability and reduce the electron transfer distance between nanoparticles and the cell membrane, which further enhanced the direct electron transfer efficiency in the click biohybrid system. Therefore, the prepared click system showed remarkable H2 yield when subjected to visible light irradiation. The new covalent click biohybrid system demonstrates significant potential as a versatile platform for solar hydrogen production or chemical synthesis.

Tian-Yu Zhu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yi-Cheng Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Chong Sha: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Said Nawab: Writing – review & editing. Jun-Ying Liu: Data curation. Yang-Chun Yong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (52170081), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Carbon Neutralization, BK20220003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2025.132410.

ce:section.ce:para:
